title: My Children Walk in the Truth
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: My Children Walk in the Truth __ 3 John 1-4 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt

outline.title: Setting the Table: Context of 3 John
subpoints: Written around AD 90 to address a leader named Diotrephes who refused hospitality to traveling evangelists
Likely addresses elders in a church (probably Ephesus): Gaius, Diotrephes, and Demetrius
Diotrephes loved preeminence and was blocking faithful preachers; Gaius and Demetrius were resisting him
The theme of loving truth from 2 John continues: in 2 John, the problem was receiving false teachers; in 3 John, it is refusing faithful teachers

outline.title: Caring for One Another Body and Soul (vv. 2-3)
subpoints: John prays for Gaius's health and prosperity as well as the prosperity of his soul
We are often better at praying for physical needs; the New Testament's great prayers are mostly for the soul
Most of Paul's prayers in the epistles are for spiritual strengthening, comprehension of love, and fullness of God
We need each other to share both physical and spiritual requests; we need provision for our souls as well as our bodies

outline.title: The Joy of Seeing Brothers and Sisters Walk in the Truth (vv. 3-4)
subpoints: John rejoiced greatly when visitors reported that Gaius was living faithfully according to the Word of God
The truth of God has become Gaius's own truth — not a personal version, but God's objective truth subjectively appropriated
Seven reasons from 2 John: Jesus is the truth; the Spirit is the spirit of truth; truth sets us free; truth leads to true worship
What excites John is not Gaius's outward success but his spiritual growth — and this should excite us about each other

outline.title: Two Applications: Rejoice in Spiritual Growth and Provide for Your Children's Souls
subpoints: Application 1: Spiritual growth in each other must bring us great joy — when a brother grows in Christ, we all grow
The toilet tank illustration: the water level rises for all as each drop fills the tank; we grow together into the fullness of Christ
Application 2: What provisions are you making for the souls of your children?
Children of parents who don't love the church will generally not love Jesus — pass on love for Christ, His Word, and His body

summary:
This sermon on 3 John 1-4 is the second message in a series on 2 and 3 John, preached in the afternoon session of the same day as the 2 John sermon. Pastor Lyro sets the table by explaining the occasion of the letter: around AD 90, the Apostle John writes to Gaius because the leader Diotrephes — who loves preeminence — is refusing to provide hospitality to traveling evangelists and is even threatening to put out of the church those who would. John writes to encourage Gaius, to address Diotrephes, and to commend Demetrius, in preparation for John's own personal visit to settle the matter.

The sermon identifies two main lessons from verses 1-4. First, Christian care extends to both body and soul. John prays for Gaius's physical health and prosperity, but his greater concern is the prosperity of Gaius's soul. The point for the congregation is that our prayer lists often reflect our priorities — abundant prayers for physical ailments, fewer prayers for spiritual growth. But the great prayers of the New Testament (particularly Paul's in the epistles) are overwhelmingly for the inner man: strengthening by the Spirit, comprehension of the love of Christ, being filled with all the fullness of God.

Second, John's great joy comes from hearing that Gaius is walking in the truth — and this should be the greatest joy of every Christian regarding the brothers and sisters around them. Pastor Lyro explains that the truth has become Gaias's own truth: not "your truth" in the modern relativistic sense, but God's objective truth has been subjectively appropriated by Gaius — it has become his very own. This is what sanctification looks like. The sermon closes with two applications: spiritual growth in our brothers and sisters should bring us great joy (using the illustration of a toilet tank where all the water rises together), and parents should make intentional spiritual provision for their children — family devotions, prayer, modeling love for the Word and for the church.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: 3 John 1-15

If you're able, you're remaining standing as we read from 3 John — well, there's only one chapter. So 3 John, verse 1. This is the Word of our Lord:

"The elder to the beloved Gaius, whom I love in truth. Beloved, I pray that you may prosper in all things and be in health, just as your soul prospers. For I rejoiced greatly when brethren came and testified of the truth that is in you, just as you walk in the truth. I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth. Beloved, you do faithfully whatever you do for the brethren and for strangers, who have borne witness of your love before the church. If you send them forward on their journey in a manner worthy of God, you will do well. Because they went forth for His name's sake, taking nothing from the Gentiles. We therefore ought to receive such, that we may become fellow workers for the truth. I wrote to the church, but Diotrephes, who loves to have the preeminence among them, does not receive us. Therefore, if I come, I will call to mind his deeds which he does, prating against us with malicious words. And not content with that, he himself does not receive the brethren, and forbids those who wish to, putting them out of the church. Beloved, do not imitate what is evil, but what is good. He who does good is of God, but he who does evil has not seen God. Demetrius has a good testimony from all, and from the truth itself. And we also bear witness, and you know that our testimony is true. I had many things to write, but I do not wish to write to you with pen and ink; but I hope to see you shortly, and we shall speak face to face. Peace to you. Our friends greet you. Greet the friends by name."

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Glorious God, we thank You for Your Word. We pray that we would take heed to it. Enable me to speak in a way that's helpful to Your people. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen. Please be seated.

## Setting the Table: The Occasion of 3 John

The Apostle John wrote this short epistle — less than 300 words in the original language — around AD 90. It came to his attention that the leadership of a particular little church — likely the church of Ephesus — was refusing to offer hospitality to traveling evangelists who were coming through town. So he writes this letter to try to straighten things out, in preparation for his coming to visit them in person.

It is likely that the letter he refers to — that he says he wrote to the church, which Diotrephes did not let be read — is the letter we considered this morning: 2 John. Diotrephes did not let the church receive it. So John writes 3 John to Gaius to let him know that there is this letter out there which should be read to the church — but that John is going to come in person and settle things with Diotrephes and the rest of the church.

John identifies himself as "the elder." As we saw with 2 John, that is not because he's an old man — though he is — but because he speaks with the authority of Christ behind him.

The recipient is Gaius — a very common name in the first century. Impossible to identify who specifically this Gaius was. It's likely that he was a member with influence in the church, and likely an elder — because it seems that Gaius, Diotrephes, and Demetrius were all elders in the church. And the session was not functioning well because Diotrephes — this one who loved preeminence — was taking over and controlling all things, fighting with the other elders.

So we have this session: Diotrephes shutting down Demetrius, trying to push his own agenda. Gaius seems to be in agreement with Demetrius. John writes 3 John to try to settle the situation.

The theme of loving truth that we saw in 2 John continues here. Now, in 2 John, the problem was the *reception* of false teachers. But here in 3 John, the issue is the *refusal* to provide hospitality to faithful teachers — especially by Diotrephes.

In the first century, hotels were more like brothels than the hotels we think of today. Any person of reputation would not stay in such places. They depended on other people's hospitality as they traveled. That's what Mary and Joseph seem to have done — knocking at the house of a relative who was already too full in Bethlehem, which is why they had to stay in the adjacent animal shelter.

As the gospel went throughout the world, these itinerant, traveling preachers of the gospel needed the help of established churches as jumping-off points to the next place where there was no church. And Diotrephes was saying the church could not host those people — seemingly because he was afraid that somehow he would have to share his glory with the visiting evangelists.

## Caring for One Another Body and Soul (vv. 2-3)

Now we can start considering this letter. The first thing it teaches us is that we are to care for one another — body and soul. Look at verses 2 and 3:

"Beloved, I pray that you may prosper in all things and be in health, just as your soul prospers. For I rejoiced greatly when brethren came and testified of the truth that is in you, just as you walk in the truth."

John cared for Gaius's body and for Gaius's soul. He prayed for good health — and he prayed for his soul as well.

We get the body part. But sometimes we forget the soul part. I think our prayer lists are a testimony to that — we have almost an ICU roster with all the different diseases we're praying for. And often we're not necessarily praying for each other's souls. We don't feel like we can share our spiritual struggles and spiritual requests with each other.

But most of the prayers we find in the New Testament, when someone is praying for someone else, have to do with the souls of the brethren. That's particularly true in Paul's writings. For example, in Ephesians 3:14-19, Paul says: "For this reason I bow my knees to the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ... that He would grant you, according to the riches of His glory, to be strengthened with might through His Spirit in the inner man, that Christ may dwell in your hearts through faith, that you, being rooted and grounded in love, may be able to comprehend with all the saints what is the width and length and depth and height — to know the love of Christ which passes knowledge, that you may be filled with all the fullness of God."

It's not that we don't pray for the body — but we pray for the soul as well. I emphasize the soul because I think we have the body covered, the physical. We need also to make provision for the souls of the brethren.

And not only that — the spiritual growth of Gaius excited John. In verse 3: "I rejoiced greatly when brethren came and testified of the truth that is in you." Some people who had visited Gaius's church also visited John and told him that Gaius was living a faithful life according to the Word of God. And that got John excited. He greatly rejoiced that Gaius was doing well spiritually — walking in the truth, living according to the Word of God.

This is simply the process and progress of sanctification that we all should be going through. What was going on with Gaius is not something extraordinary. That's what should be happening in all of us, as day by day we become more like Jesus Christ. And we should all be getting more excited about how our brother and sister is growing in Jesus Christ.

I know that sometimes I can be a bit of a — I am a realist. Some people say I'm a pessimist, but I am a realist. And often when I give feedback to somebody who's taught or preached, I say: "Look, I'm going to say three things" — which means there are 97 things that are good that I'm not saying. I'm only saying the three things I think you need to work on. And I'm really good at noticing those.

God says: notice what's good, the work of grace in each other's lives. Be excited by it. John rejoiced greatly. We tend to be more able to find what's wrong with other people's lives than to see the work of grace in people's lives. Let's get excited about the good that God is doing in the brethren, instead of just finding reasons to be upset or complain.

## The Joy of Seeing Brothers and Sisters Walk in the Truth (vv. 3-4)

In verse 3, the expression "the truth that is in you" literally means "your truth" — the brothers testified of his truth. Now this is not a personal version of the truth in the "live your truth" sense. Rather, it means that the truth of God has become Gaius's own truth. God's objective truth has been subjectively appropriated by Gaius. His relationship to the truth is not abstract — it has become his.

The second thing the brethren reported is that they told John of Gaius's love for the traveling teachers. In verse 6, the same brothers say: "who have borne witness of your love before the church." Gaius's love for the preachers of the Word was not a hidden love. The whole church knew that Gaius loved those who proclaimed God's Word and would do whatever he could to make sure these teachers had what they needed to continue their ministry. And John is excited that somebody in the church has that heart.

John stops in commenting on this particular verse — and someone once said, since testimony can be borne only to what has been seen, it is clear that Gaius was a transparent, open Christian who was letting his light shine and not hiding it.

Are we doing our part to make sure that those who are proclaiming the gospel are provided for? Are we receiving the message of the cross from them? Are we excited about the preaching of the gospel of Jesus Christ?

It's become popular to say the mission field is everywhere. That is true. But when you think of the mission field as everywhere, guess where the mission field ends up being? Nowhere. Because we don't usually live intentionally as if we're in the mission field. And there are still billions of people who have never heard the name of Jesus — and there is nobody trying to get the name of Jesus to them. Not just that they haven't heard — there is no one trying to make sure they hear.

I contacted the Orthodox Presbyterian Church's foreign missions chairman last year. I said: "Hey, we have some extra dollars we'd like to invest in missions." He said: "We don't need money. We have more money than we can spend. We need *people*." The reason they have so much money is that they don't have enough people doing the work. If you really care about the gospel, money is never the issue. There is money. But we need people.

And then John addresses in verse 4 his attitude toward his spiritual children: "I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth." That's the favorite parent verse, isn't it? John and Paul often refer to those who came to faith through their ministry as their children. That's probably the case with Gaius — he likely came to faith under the ministry of John, or at the very least, John spent a lot of time discipling him.

And John as a spiritual father and elder in the church rejoices in the spiritual being of those under his care. That's what your elders want for you. We want all of us to grow together in the fullness of our Head, the Lord Jesus Christ. We rejoice greatly in your spiritual growth.

## Two Applications

Let me finish with two applications. The first is consistent with the meaning of the passage. The second the passage may remind us of.

**First application**: Spiritual growth in each other must bring us great joy. There are two reasons for that. One: when a brother or sister is growing spiritually, it means they are growing closer to Christ — and that should excite us. The second reason is that spiritual growth among God's people benefits all of God's people in the whole of the church.

Have you ever looked inside the exposed tank of a toilet? That's my analogy of the church — the exposed tank. Not the bottom part, but the water-holding tank. Do you ever see how the mechanism works? Water pours in. As the water fills the entire tank, the little buoy goes up. Water comes in, the buoy goes up. And if the buoy is working correctly, when it reaches a certain level, the water shuts off. But the little buoy doesn't go up unless the water occupies the *entire* space — not just this much here. The entire level has to rise. Every drop of water has to come in for the entire level to rise.

That's the church of Jesus Christ. We only grow as a body as everyone is growing. So there's a somewhat self-oriented reason for excitement about growth in Christ: if each one grows in Christ, I grow in Christ too. Because we grow together into the fullness of our Head, the Lord Jesus Christ.

**Second application**: What brings you great joy as far as your children are concerned? John says: "I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth." Is your great joy found in their spiritual wellbeing?

We make provisions for the body: health insurance, education, sports. How about spiritual provision? What spiritual provision are you making for your children? Are you instilling in them love for Jesus? You can't do that if you're not loving Jesus yourself. Are you instilling in them love for Jesus's body, the church? I'm very convinced that when parents don't love the church — generally speaking — the children will not love Jesus. That's how it works generationally.

How about your own spiritual disciplines? Do your children see you reading your Bible? Praying? Rejoicing in that? There is a sense in which we're supposed to go into our closet to pray — but don't close the door all the way. Let it open a crack so the kids can see that you're praying, that you rejoice in it. Do you conduct family devotions? Do you bring them before the throne of God in prayer?

There are so many testimonies in church history where great heroes of the faith attribute their salvation to the faithful mothers who prayed for them. Augustine is one — he attributed his salvation to the prayers of Monica his mother. Many, many souls have been won on the knees of their mothers. Do you bring your children before the throne of grace in prayer and wrestle with God? "I'm not going to let You go until You've blessed my children."

The Christian life must be a life of growth. As we grow, we become like Jesus. And we rejoice that those around us are also growing. And together, we become more like our Head, the Lord Jesus Christ, in the Spirit together.

Lord, we thank You for Your goodness to us. We thank You that You put us in the body, and that together we are growing. We thank You for all the evidences of grace that we see in our congregation. Help us to recognize them and to rejoice in them. We pray that You continue to work in us, that together we will grow from glory to glory, until the Lord Jesus Christ. In His name we pray. Amen.
